Tests received by maloo, run on CentOS release 6.4/x86_64: (https://maloo.whamcloud.com/test_sessions/c3ca4ed0-4f05-11e3-b578-52540035b04c).  Ran 3 tests.  1 tests failed: sanity-lfsck.
Patch Set 14: Looks good to me, approved

Patch Set 14:

Looks like sanity-lfsck failed for ZFS?

Patch Set 14:

> Patch Set 14:
> Looks like sanity-lfsck failed for ZFS?

It is performance issue. Seems the ZFS-based iteration is too slow than the expectation. The prefetching mechanism needs to be improved?

Patch Set 14: I would prefer that you didn't submit this

From the sanity-lfsck test results, the zfs-based iteration speed is too slow. Even if it is under VM environment, it should not be.

I am considering that whether we should use a dedicated thread to prefetch objects to cache in advance (just like ldiskfs does), instead of the LFSCK engine thread itself does that?

There is lfsck-performance.sh, we can add "testlist=lfsck-performance,sanity-lfsck" in the commit message to see the full speed of zfs-based iteration. If it is really too slow, then either improve the prefetching mechanism, or fix test_9b speed limit to match the zfs-based iteration and without breaking ldiskfs-based one.

Patch Set 14:

I reported before that on a local system I got ~5K objects/second, it doesn't look very slow.. I'd like to see raw numbers to make sure.
Uploaded patch set 15.

Patch Set 15:

Build Started http://build.whamcloud.com/job/lustre-reviews/19665/

Patch Set 15: Verified

Build Successful 
 
http://build.whamcloud.com/job/lustre-reviews/19665/ : SUCCESS

Patch Set 15: Looks good to me, but someone else must approve

(1 inline comment)

This looks reasonable to me.  Regarding the observed performance of dmu_object_next() it is generally quite fast.  However, there are some workloads where it may be sluggish due to having to read multiple dnode blocks from disk.  Prakash and I have looked in to optimizing those cases but it was never clear this was a really a problem in practice.  It would be good to know if Lustre is legitimately hitting these issues   so we could properly prioritize speeding up dmu_object_next() in the ZFS code.

Patch Set 20:

Does it make sense for the OSD-internal objects to get special FIDs or a flag that can be can be filtered by the iterator before returning it to the caller? I agree that it doesn't make sense for the upper layers to get direct access to internal objects just to discard them. 

That said, it would be up to the OSD code to check and set the needed flags on the object so that it is not returned. That could be done as part of the initial setup at mount time, before scrub is started.

Patch Set 20:

That is the current osd-ldiskfs does which has filter out those local objects before returning to up layer LFSCK via the iteration. But Alex though that it is the LFSCK itself duty to do the filter.

Patch Set 20:

internal objects are not supposed to have FID. for example, /O does not.

Patch Set 20:

But some local objects have FIDs, such as "fld"/"seq_ctl"/"seq_srv", and so on.

Patch Set 20:

Should the upper-layer LFSCK skip FIDs in FID_SEQ_LOCAL?

Patch Set 20:

Originally, all the external invisible objects have been filtered out by osd-ldiskfs before the iterator returning. So the LFSCK does not check whether the FID is local or not. But if we want to make the LFSCK to filter something by itself, such check is necessary.

Patch Set 20:

> But some local objects have FIDs, such as "fld"/"seq_ctl"/"seq_srv", and so on.

and those are normal OSD objects, why OSD should bother with filtering, etc? especially if it's many different OSDs.

Patch Set 20:

Alex: I did not opposite to filter the special objects/FIDs by LFSCK itself. But as I commented before, I prefer to do that without affecting current LFSCK phase II patches.

Patch Set 20:

sure, now tell me what objects should I filter out :) ideally - sequences.

Patch Set 20:

I am working on the patch to filter out special FIDs/objects by LFSCK itself. So do you want to make some workable solution in your patch temporarily?

Patch Set 20:

Alex: Here is the patch (http://review.whamcloud.com/#/c/9186/) for LFSCK to filter out special objects. It has passed all sanity-scrub/sanity-lfsck tests. You can try your patch against this one.

Patch Set 20:

I've got a new version which seem to be working well (except test 10), will push it as soon as FanYong's patch to LFSCK is committed (do not want to risk to push on top). in a meanwhile we can discuss how to address that lookup(..) issue.

Patch Set 26:

Nathaniel, have you tested this latest patch on ZFS?  It seems that the custom test to run with FSTYPE=zfs didn't run, so while it appears ready to land I don't want to land it if this will cause a regression with ZFS.

Patch Set 26:

It looks like the custom review-zfs test failed with:

 mkfs.lustre --mgsnode=shadow-6vm12@tcp --fsname=lustre --ost --index=0 --param=sys.timeout=20 --backfstype=zfs --device-size=3217630 --reformat lustre-ost1/ost1 /dev/lvm-Role_OSS/P1
 mkfs.lustre FATAL: mkfs.lustre: Unable to create backing store: No such file or directory
 mkfs.lustre FATAL: Unable to create vdev /dev/lvm-Role_OSS/P1 (2)

I'm not sure if that indicates a problem with the patch or the test environment.
Change has been successfully cherry-picked as cf753f6b4bdc267b5169e97293f69b5362b826de

commit.subject: LU-3489 osd-zfs: object iterator support
commit.message:
LU-3489 osd-zfs: object iterator support

a trivial object iterator for ZFS using dmu_object_next().
the iterator skips all objects with no LMA EA. It does
implement simple prefetching algoright. It also enables
few tests in sanity-lfsck to be run with ZFS.

Test-Parameters: mdtfilesystemtype=zfs \
    ostfilesystemtype=zfs mdsfilesystemtype=zfs \
    testlist=sanity-scrub,sanity-lfsck
Signed-off-by: Alex Zhuravlev <alexey.zhuravlev@intel.com>
Change-Id: Icf02342ac351a163e23575963d3366761c59b02b
Reviewed-on: http://review.whamcloud.com/7149
Reviewed-by: Andreas Dilger <andreas.dilger@intel.com>
Tested-by: Jenkins
